perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, explicitly excluding: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method;
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding scientific and technical information from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract contradicts the common understanding and legal precedent regarding trade secrets and confidential business information, leading to potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets as including scientific and technical information. By excluding this information, the modified contract provides less protection than the federal law intends, potentially hindering the ability to claim trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes 'any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' The Law Explanation states that the DTSA defines trade secrets as including scientific and technical information, and the Perturbation Explanation indicates this exclusion contradicts legal precedent regarding trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and its codification at 18 U.S.C. § 1836, which, as understood from the Law Explanation, includes scientific and technical information within its definition of trade secrets. By explicitly excluding this type of information, the modified contract undermines the protections intended by trade secret law, creating a direct conflict. The absence of a second snippet does not diminish the contradiction established by the first snippet, Law Explanation, and Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shal l be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyrigh t or other intelle c- tual property right.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party, except where the Receiving Party makes substantial improvements. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyrigh t or other intelle c- tual property right.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity regarding ownership of confidential information when 'substantial improvements' are made by the Receiving Party. This could conflict with established intellectual property law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Copyright law dictates ownership of derivative works. The phrase 'substantial improvements' is vague and could be interpreted as creating a derivative work, potentially assigning ownership to the Receiving Party contrary to the Disclosing Party's intent and established copyright principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i

The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.—When an individual author’s ownership of a copyright, or of any of the exclusive rights under a copyright, has not previously been transferred voluntarily by that individual author, no action by any governmental body or other offici
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that could contradict copyright law regarding ownership of derivative works. The original text clearly states that all confidential information remains the property of the disclosing party. The changed text introduces an exception: 'except where the Receiving Party makes substantial improvements.' This exception is vague and potentially conflicts with 17 U.S. Code § 201, which states that copyright vests initially in the author of the work, or in the case of a work made for hire, the employer, unless otherwise agreed. The 'substantial improvements' could be construed as creating a derivative work, and without a clear agreement, the receiving party might claim ownership, contradicting the disclosing party's initial ownership as initially intended in the original contract.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect for only 1 year after termination of this agreement, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligation to only 1 year could be insufficient to protect trade secrets, which under certain legal frameworks require indefinite protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA aims to protect trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. By limiting the confidentiality period to one year, even if the information remains a trade secret, it contradicts the principles of the UTSA, potentially limiting the scope of legal protection for the disclosing party beyond the specified term.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates an indefinite confidentiality period, while the changed text limits it to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states this limitation could be insufficient to protect trade secrets requiring indefinite protection. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Limiting the confidentiality period contradicts the UTSA's principles. The modified text undermines the potential for indefinite protection required to maintain trade secret status, thus creating a legal contradiction under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to principles of equity. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'laws of [jurisdiction]' with 'principles of equity' introduces uncertainty. While equity is part of the legal system, it's not a defined body of statutory law like state or federal codes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that contracts usually specify a particular jurisdiction's laws for interpretation. 'Principles of equity' are abstract and lack the concrete guidance provided by statutory law, conflicting with established legal practices for contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies interpretation 'by the laws of [choose the applicable law]' which implies a specific, defined legal code. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity', which is a broader, less defined concept. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws dictates that contracts usually specify a particular jurisdiction's laws for interpretation, and that 'principles of equity' lack the concrete guidance provided by statutory law. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The scraped law snippet defines conflict of laws as arising from differences between jurisdictions, implying that identifiable bodies of law must exist to create such a conflict. Principles of equity, lacking such a clearly defined structure, would complicate the resolution of conflict of laws.

perturbation.original_text: 3.10 The Parties agree that the Disclosing Party wi ll suffer irreparable damage if its Co n- fidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injun c- tive relief against a threatened brea ch or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such a breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.10 The Parties agree that the Disclosing Party wi ll suffer irreparable damage only if highly sensitive Confidential Information such as source code is made public. The Disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injun c- tive relief against a threatened brea ch or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such a breach, an award of actual damages only from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting 'irreparable damage' to only 'highly sensitive Confidential Information such as source code' and removing 'exemplary damages' significantly limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the purpose of an NDA to protect all confidential information, not just a subset.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA allows for both injunctive relief and damages (including exemplary damages in cases of willful and malicious misappropriation) to compensate for trade secret misappropriation. This revision limits the scope of what constitutes 'irreparable harm' and removes the possibility of exemplary damages, contradicting the remedies intended by the UTSA and potentially undervaluing the Disclosing Party's confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits 'irreparable damage' to 'highly sensitive Confidential Information such as source code' and removes 'exemplary damages'. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA allows for both injunctive relief and damages (including exemplary damages). By limiting the definition of what constitutes irreparable harm and eliminating exemplary damages, the changed text contradicts the remedies intended by the UTSA. While the scraped snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation clearly states the legal obligation, which the Changed Text directly contradicts.
